coredata.dc:title: Understanding the high chemi-catalytic reactivity of graphene quantum dots to rapidly generate reactive oxygen species
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Shuhan
Beiyuan, Jingzi
Huang, Liyun
Cao, Xiaocong
Deng, Maojun
Lv, Daofei
Chen, Xin
Liu, Defei
Luo, Dongmei
Yuan, Wenbing
coredata.dc:description:
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered favorable consideration as an advanced metal-free base material for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). Recently, it is of great interest to develop new catalytic reactions for the GQDs. However, a rapid and simultaneous generation of large amounts of multiple ROS through GQDs is still challenging. Herein, we firstly prepare N-GQDs (nitrogen-doped GQDs) with abundant irregular structure defects and rich reactive functional groups (CO, CO, OCO, and CN) in a mechanochemical method, which can simultaneously generate a large number of ROS including 1O2,
O2−, and
OH in the presence of in the presence of acid (either inorganic or organic acid) without oxygen in dark. At the same time, the N-GQDs were reduced to N-rGQDs (nitrogen-doped reduced GQDs) accompanied by CO2 release. The process and mechanism of ROS generation of the N-GQDs was extensively discussed. The obtained N-GQDs can efficiently catalyze the hydrothermal synthesis of MIL-101, a well-known metal-organic framework, which suggested the N-GQDs can be widely applied in different area to generate ROS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Reactive oxygen species
Nitrogen-doped GQDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism studies on acid-induced ROS and CO2 release of N-GQDs
Catalytic synthesis of a metal-organic framework: MIL-101(Cr)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: GC–MS spectra of N-GQDs-300 in presence of HCl (12 M): (a) GC spectrum, (b) MS spectrum.

High reactivity of mechanochemically synthesized N-GQDs in acid. (a) Optical photographs of the reaction process of N-GQDs-300 in the presence of 0.1 mL HCl (12 M). Black precipitate of N-rGQDs-300 was dispersed in DMF after centrifugation. (b-d) TEM, HRTEM and AFM images of N-rGQDs-300-precipitate. The inset of (b) and (d) are size distribution of the dots and height pro-file of the selected area respectively. (e-g) TEM, HRTEM and AFM images of N-rGQDs-300-solution. The inset of (e) and (g) are size distribution of the dots and height profile of the selected area respectively. (h-i) High-resolution XPS from survey and C1s for samples N-rGQDs-300, N-rGQDs-300-precipitate, and N-rGQDs-300-solution.

ESR spectra of N-GQDs-300 with and without addition of concentrated HCl to detect (a) singlet oxygen by using TEMP as a trapping agent in ultrapure water, (b) superoxide radical by using DMPO as a trapping agent in ultrapure water, (c) hydroxyl radical by using DMPO as a trapping agent in DMSO. All the above ESR spectra were recorded after sulfuric acid addition for 5 min. (d) UV spectra of N-GQDs-300 in the presence of KI solution with and without adding H2SO4 (10%) to show the evidence of the existence of H2O2 through I3− formation.

(a) Optical photographs of N-GQDs-300 aqueous solution in the presence of 0.2 mL 30 % H2O2. (b-d) TEM, HRTEM and AFM images of the precipitate part of N-GQDs-300 reacted with H2O2. The inset of (b) and (d) are size distribution of the dots and height profile of the selected area respectively. (e-g) TEM, HRTEM and AFM images of the aqueous solution part of N-GQDs-300 reacted with H2O2. The inset of (e) and (g) are size distribution of the dots and height profile of the selected area respectively.
Solvothermal synthesis of MIL-101(Cr) in presence of GQDs at 220 00B0 for an hour in water.
Proposed reaction process of these mechanochemically-obtained N-GQDs in the presence of acid and N-rGQDs-300-solution.

head.ce:title: Understanding the high chemi-catalytic reactivity of graphene quantum dots to rapidly generate reactive oxygen species

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Hainan University, Haikou 570228, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered favorable consideration as an advanced metal-free base material for generating reactive oxygen species (ROS). Recently, it is of great interest to develop new catalytic reactions for the GQDs. However, a rapid and simultaneous generation of large amounts of multiple ROS through GQDs is still challenging. Herein, we firstly prepare N-GQDs (nitrogen-doped GQDs) with abundant irregular structure defects and rich reactive functional groups (CO, CO, OCO, and CN) in a mechanochemical method, which can simultaneously generate a large number of ROS including 1O2,
O2−, and
OH in the presence of in the presence of acid (either inorganic or organic acid) without oxygen in dark. At the same time, the N-GQDs were reduced to N-rGQDs (nitrogen-doped reduced GQDs) accompanied by CO2 release. The process and mechanism of ROS generation of the N-GQDs was extensively discussed. The obtained N-GQDs can efficiently catalyze the hydrothermal synthesis of MIL-101, a well-known metal-organic framework, which suggested the N-GQDs can be widely applied in different area to generate ROS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Reactive oxygen species (ROS) including single oxygen (1O2), hydroxyl radical (
OH), and superoxide anion (O2
−) are highly reactive molecules derived from oxygen. Due to their high relative abundance and negligible environmental toxicity and naturally generated properties, the ROS plays an important role in medical and chemical area (Nowaczyk et al., 2022; Xiong and Tang, 2021). Furthermore, it can also be catalytically generated by various substances and materials with the induction of external conditions (e.g. light irradiation (Mitiche et al., 2022) and Gamma ray irradiation (Sharma et al., 2017)). These ROS-generated techniques were also widely applied for degradation of pollutants (Ahmed et al., 2022; Meng et al., 2022; Tan et al., 2022), fine chemical synthesis (Fujiwara et al., 2014, p. 2; Kouridaki et al., 2015, 2013; Tian et al., 2021) and accelerated crystallization (Yang et al., 2019).

To date, several types of materials such as black phosphorus nanosheet (Wang et al., 2022), graphene (Yan et al., 2022), and nanodots (Liu et al., 2021) have been tested as catalysts or catalyst supports for the ROS generation. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are one of the carbonaceous materials. Owing to their small size, unique physicochemical properties, and biocatalytic behaviors, the GQDs have been recognized as a promising and advanced metal-free base material to generate ROS in medical and catalysis area (Sweetman et al., 2019). The structural defects and functional groups on GQDs caused by different synthesis methods have great effects on the ROS generation (Chen et al., 2018). Normally, the GQDs can generate only one or two ROS under varied conditions. In most cases, the GQDs with surface-located functional groups (OH, CH, and CC) can self-generate 1O2 with UV-light (λ = 365 nm) irradiation (Jovanović et al., 2015; Santos et al., 2021; Tabish et al., 2018). In addition, studies found that the defect-rich GQD synthesized by an electrochemical method can also generate
OH with the induction of H2O2 (Wang et al., 2021). Lately, a pyrrole N and pyridine N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) prepared via a solvothermal method (by EtOH and dimethylformamide (DMF)) can generate ROS (including 1O2 and
OH) with ultrasound induction (Yang et al., 2021).

In our previous study, we developed a solvent-free mechanochemical method for the preparation of N-GQDs with the highest solubility (31 mg mL−1) in water, which exhibits white-light emission and broad excitation-dependent full-color photoluminescence (Deng et al., 2020). Notably, this approach can cause rich irregular structure defects and afford large amounts of reactive functional groups (CO, CO, OCO, and CN) onto the GQDs. It is hypothesized that the unique structures of the as-obtained N-GQDs can generate large amounts of ROS. Therefore, in current study, we thoroughly investigated the generation mechanisms of ROS by the N-GQDs, and discovered for the first time that it can generate a large amount of ROS including 1O2,
O2−, and
OH only in the presence of acid. To the best of our knowledge, there are rarely GQDs can largely generate multiple ROS without further induction, such as ultrasound, UV, etc. We also studied its catalytical capacity via a hydrothermal synthesis of a well-known metal-organic framework: MIL-101(Cr).

In this study, we used N-GQDs which were prepared by an efficient mechanochemical approach via the ball mill at a rate of 300 rpm, referred as N-GQDs-300. The detailed preparation can be referred to in our previous work (Deng et al., 2020), which is also provided in Supporting Information S.1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were collected on an F20 S-TWIN electron microscope (Tecnai G2, FEI Company), using a 200 kV accelerating voltage. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a NanoScope-IIIa MultiMode-AFM (Veeco, USA). The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) was performed on a Hitachi S4800. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI XPS spectrometer. XRD patterns were obtained with a Bruker AXS D8 advanced X-ray powder diffractometer, equipped with Cu Kα radiation (γ = 0.15405 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a TENSOR 27 spectrometer by using KBr pellets. Raman spectra were recorded on a LabRAM HR800 in plus laser Raman spectrometer with λexc = 633 nm. A model T6 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., ltd., Beijing, China) was used for scanning the absorption spectrum. A Bruker EMX ESR spectrometer (Billerica, MA) was used for all the Electron Spin Resonance (ESR) spectroscopy measurements at room temperature under dark, and spectra were recorded after H2SO4 (10 %) reacted with N-GQDs for 5 min.

DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxid) and TEMP (2,2,6,6-Tetramethylpiperidine) were used as the spin trap reagents were purchased from Sigma Aldrich Co., ltd. All other chemicals and solvents were supplied by Guangzhou Chemical Inc. and used without further purification.

To verify the quick and spontaneous catalytical capacity of the N-GQDs, we use it to synthesize a chromium terephthalate framework, MIL-101. The detailed experimental design is presented in Supporting Information S.2.

Previously, we surprisingly found that the N-GQDs can release gases under acidic conditions, which could be contributed to the ROS-release catalytical reaction. Therefore, we further investigated N-GQDs under different acidic conditions in the current study. Interestingly, we found that numerous bubbles were rapidly released upon the addition of HCl (12 M, 0.1 mL) to the N-GQDs-300 (Video S1 in the Supporting Information). In addition to hydrochloric acid, we also found that sulphuric acid, nitric acid, formic acid, or acetic acid, can also react with the N-GQDs and promote the rapid generation of CO2. Subjecting the released gas to GC–MS analysis revealed the concurrent formation of CO2 (the Im
/
z = 44.0 mass ion currents, Fig. 1 ) with the chemical transformation of N-GQDs in the presence of HCl. Apart from observing the rapid release of CO2 from the N-GQDs in the presence of acids, we also observed quick appearance of a large amount of black precipitate (Fig. 2 a), which is named N-rGQDs-precipitate (N-rGQDs: nitrogen-doped reduced graphene quantum dots). Meanwhile, the color of the aqueous N-GQD solution changed from golden yellow to yellow. After separating the above mixture through centrifugation, we obtained N-rGQDs-precipitate and an aqueous phase (N-rGQDs-solution). The black precipitate was found to show good dispersion in DMF, even after 6 months.

Fig. 2b-d shows the TEM, HR-TEM, and AFM images of the N-rGQDs-300-precipitate, which was dispersed in DMF. Fig. 2e-g shows the TEM, HR-TEM, and AFM images of the yellow aqueous phase N-rGQDs-300-solution. The precipitate and aqueous phase were identified as N-GQDs with sizes smaller than 5 nm. The particle size of the precipitate phase was mainly concentrated at 3.0 nm and that of the aqueous phase was concentrated at 3.8 nm. Moreover, both precipitate and the aqueous phases contained graphene sheets with less than 5- or 3-layered graphene sheets, respectively (Deng et al., 2020).

The XPS results showed that the corresponding O-containing groups changed before and after the addition of acid. Compared with the O/C ratio (63 %) of the initial N-GQDs-30023, those of the precipitate and aqueous phase were 42 % and 56 %, respectively (Table S1). The oxygen content, especially for the precipitate phase, is much lower. Indeed, this reaction is similar to the reduction process for the synthesis of N-rGQDs (Pei et al., 2010; Pei and Cheng, 2012; Zhang et al., 2015). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2i) illustrates the changes in the carbon chemical environment from N-GQDs-300 to N-rGQDs-300. The comparison of the functional groups (CC, CN, CO, CO, and OCO) between N-GQDs-300-precipitate and N-rGQDs-300 revealed that the percentage of CC significantly increased from 49 % to 74 %. Correspondingly, those of CO and CO decreased from 24 % to 5 % and from 20 % to 13 %, respectively (Table S2). For N-rGQDs-300-solution, the content of the above-mentioned functional groups decreased to a smaller degree than the N-rGQDs-300-precipitate. Given the strong oxidizing properties of ROS and H2O2, we hypothesized that the oxygen-containing functional groups (CO or COH) on the surface of N-GQDs were oxidized to CO2, which will be further discussed in Section 3.2.

The comparisons of Raman spectra of the N-GQDs before and after acid addition reveal the change in electronic conjugation state. The G band peak is associated with the tangential in-plane stretching vibration of the CC bonds within the pristine graphitic surface of GQDs, whereas the D band peak is associated with surface structural disorder arising from any surface defect sites that interrupt the sp2 carbon structure (Zhang et al., 2015). As depicted in Fig. S1, after acid addition, the ratio of ID/IG significantly increased, and the defect density of N-GQDs-300 was found as 1.23, and those of N-rGQDs-300-solution and N-rGQDs-300-precipitate as 1.42 and 1.36 respectively. More defects were formed in N-GQDs-300 after the addition of HCl (12 M, 0.1 mL). This result opposed the observed reduction of the oxygen-containing functionalities (i.e., defect sites) but was consistent with the Raman data on the reduction of GQDs via NaBH4 (Zhang et al., 2015) and GO by hydrazine (Pei and Cheng, 2012) and hydrohalic acid (Pei et al., 2010). In the Raman spectra of the parent N-GQDs-300, the d-band was attributed to the sp3 carbon atoms associated with oxygen-containing functional groups. The chemical transformation of N-GQDs-300 in the presence of acid, led not only to the reduction of carbon atoms associated with oxygen-containing functional groups but also to a significant degree of fragmentation.

Beyond these results, we found that the chemical reactivity of the N-GQDs is dependent on the pH of the aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. S2a, two absorption peaks at 230 nm and 310 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs-300 gradually decayed as acidity increased from pH = 7 to 1 (Jeon et al., 2013). Besides, a new peak at 210 nm appeared at pH = 3. The absorption intensity of the entire spectrum, especially in the region below 350 nm, dramatically decreased, hinting that the N-GQDs-300 might be reduced and the aromatic structure might be gradually restored (Hou et al., 2015). In water solutions (pH = 7–12, Fig. S2b), the intensities of the two absorption peaks at 230 and 310 nm did not obviously change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism studies on acid-induced ROS and CO2 release of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanism that underlies the fast release of CO2 with the reduction-like reaction of the mechanochemically obtained N-GQDs in the presence of acid were discussed here. We noticed that graphene oxide readily led to the production of CO2 and reduced GO (rGO) species under sunlight exposure (Gengler et al., 2013). The mechanisms of catalytic O2 generating ROS, including 1O2,
O2 −, and
OH, during CO2 production and reduction with a photosensitizer have been well investigated (Ge et al., 2014; Jovanović et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016). In view of similar reaction results such as CO2 release and reduction-like reaction between our work and the above photocatalytic work on GO, we speculate that acid may promote N-GQDs to chemicatalytically generate ROS and CO2. Therefore, electron-spin resonance experiments were designed and performed on the N-GQDs-300/H+ system in dark conditions to search for the presence of these ROS when N-GQDs were reacted with acid.

When TEMP was used as a spin trap agent, the N-GQDS-300/H+ system exhibited a three-line ESR spectrum (Fig. 3 a), which suggested that 1O2 was produced as ROS. In addition, using another spin trap agent, DMPO in ultrapure water, we were able to detect
OH. As shown in Fig. 3b, the N-GQDs-300/H+ system produced characteristic signals for DMPO-
OH adducts in the ESR spectrum, suggesting that
OH did form in this GQD-H+-based catalytic O2. To detect superoxide radical
O2 −, absolute dimethyl sulfoxide was employed as the reaction medium instead of ultrapure water to act as the scavenger for
OH and prolong the half-life of
O2 −. With the addition of DMPO, the four ESR signals assigned to DMPO-
O2 − adducts were observed in the catalytic O2 oxidation with N-GQDs-300/H+ (Fig. 3c). By contrast, all these characteristic ESR signals of the above ROS were absent when acid was not added to the as-obtained N-GQDs. These results verified that energy transfer and electron transfer occurred from the GQDs to oxygen and are responsible for the sensitization of ground-state oxygen. Furthermore, H2O2 formation was detected on the N-GQDs-300 sample in the presence of H2SO4. The analysis of aqueous H2O2 at concentrations of as low as 1 μM was conveniently performed using the I3− method, which is based on the spectrophotometric determination of I3− formed when H2O2 was added to a concentrated I− solution (Klassen et al., 1994). Fig. 3d shows the absorption spectra of the N-GQDs-300/KI/H+ system. This spectrum exhibited two peaks at 288 and 353 nm, indicating that H2O2 formed when N-GQDs were under acidic conditions (Huwiler and Kohler, 1984).

Based on the above results, three ROS (1O2,
O2 −, and
OH) and H2O2 were all found to be present in the process of acid addition to the N-GQDs-300. However, which one played a more important role in this process is still unclear. Hou et al. (2016) reported the phototransformation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT) in the presence of H2O2 under simulated sunlight conditions; in this reaction, the formed CO2 strongly aggregates SWCNTs, which then precipitate. Moreover, such a photoreaction caused the SWCNTs to lose their oxygen-containing moieties. The above results are attributed to the reaction of
OH radicals that were generated from H2O2 with carboxylic acid groups on the SWCNT surface (Hou et al., 2016, 2014). However, because the ROS possess short half-lives, which are difficult to be captured, we attempted to verify the process by easily obtaining H2O2 in accordance with the above assumptions.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, when 0.2 mL of 30 % H2O2 was added to an aqueous solution of N-GQDs-300 at room temperature, numerous bubbles appeared in the cuvette 1 day later (Video S2). The results of the GC–MS analysis showed that the released gas was CO2. By prolonging the standing time, a brown precipitate appeared, and the colour of the aqueous solution became lighter. These experimental results show that the added H2O2 reacts with the carboxylic acid groupas on the as obtained N-GQDs, then result in the release of CO2.

After the analysis of the TEM, HR-TEM, AFM (Fig. 4b-g), and XPS (Fig. S3a-b and Table S1-S2) spectra of the products obtained from the N-GQDs-300/H2O2 system, we found that the process of H2O2 addition to N-GQDs is quite similar to that of acid-addition to N-GQDs; but the former reaction is much slower than the latter. Comparatively, acid greatly accelerated the production of ROS and H2O2 of N-GQDs. The ROS and H2O2 then may react with the N-GQDs to initiate the corresponding chemical structural transformation and CO2 release (Scheme 1 ). Therefore, the three ROS and H2O2 synergistically induce rapid and complicated chemical reactions in the N-GQD/H+ system.

We also found that quantum dots can also generate a large amount of ROS and carbon dioxide in a glove box, indicating that the occurrence of this process is irrelated to oxygen in the air (Video S3). The high chemicatalytic reactivity of the sub-5-nm-sized N-GQDs under acidic conditions is likely resulted from their unique chemical structures. First, during milling, the mechanical shear forces of the grinding balls resulted in the generation of edge defects and introduced oxygen-rich functional groups on the surface of the N–GQDs, directly increasing the surface defects of the nanodots. The surface defects may be associated with active sites where ROS is preferentially produced28. Second, lots of O atoms are covalently bound to GQDs formed many oxygen-containing functional groups. These oxygen-rich GQDs have the characteristics of p-type semiconductors and promote electron transfer to favour reduction. On the other hand, N atoms are covalently bound on the basal planes (for pyridinic N and quaternary N) or the edges (for pyrrolic N) of GQDs. These nitrogen-rich GQDs exhibit n-type semiconductor characteristics and promote hole transfer to favour oxidation. Modifying N-GQDs to exhibit p- and n-type conductivities may promote electron-hole pair separation and further transfer (Yeh et al., 2014). Third, these as-obtained N- and O-doped GQDs with sizes less than 5 nm may damage the original orbits and confine electrons because of the reduced size of the sp2 domain; therefore, electrons in p-type domains are easily captured by oxygen-containing functional groups when they move to the surfaces of the N-GQDs (Chen and Jafvert, 2011; Hou et al., 2015). Hence, the high oxidation-reduction reactivity under acidic conditions of the obtained N-GQDs results from their ample structural defects, abundant oxygen, and nitrogen doping, and ultra-small particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic synthesis of a metal-organic framework: MIL-101(Cr)
ce:section.ce:para:
The well-known chromium terephthalate framework, MIL-101(Cr), has a high specific surface area, huge pore volume, and possesses excellent chemical and thermal stability properties, so it has been widely studied in many fields such as selective gas adsorption/separation and heterogeneous catalysis (Zhao et al., 2015). In MIL-101, each trimeric Cr (III) octahedral cluster is also connected through one μ3-oxo ion and one OH− group (halide in some cases) (Zorainy et al., 2021). In the synthesis of MIL-101, these OH− groups and μ3-oxo ions usually come from the solvent during solvothermal treatment (Celeste et al., 2020). However, due to the slow generation rate of the OH− and μ3-oxo ion under solvothermal conditions, MIL-101 crystallization often takes a long time (more than 8 hrs), and its yield is also low. A recent work found that replacing OH− with
OH can accelerate the crystallization speed of zeolite synthesis (Feng et al., 2016). We expect that these ROS generated by N-GQDs in the presence of acid could also react with Cr ions, and consequently accelerate the MIL-101 crystallization. The results showed that MIL-101 can be efficiently obtained in the presence of N-GQDs-300 in an hour with about 50 % yield, and the BET specific surface area of the as-obtained MIL-101 increased to 3000 m2/g (Fig. 5 , Table S3). Notably, with an increased amount of N-GQDs-300, the specific surface area and yield of the obtained MIL-101 are all significantly improved. It demonstrated that the addition of N-GQDs can effectively promote the production of MIL-101, which shows their high chemi-catalytic performance.

We observed and studied a fast-chemical transformation wherein large amounts of multiple ROS, including 1O2,
O2 −, and
OH, were quickly produced with CO2 from the mechanochemically-obtained N-GQDs-300 under acidic conditions. Based on our detailed study, the generation of ROS possibly originates from: 1) edge defects and oxygen-rich functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs by the mechanical shear forces; 2) enhanced electron transfer to favour reduction and hole transfer to favour oxidation by O and N atoms of the N-GQDs; and 3) electrons of p-type domains of the N-GQDs (size < 5 mm) can be facilely captured by the oxygen-containing functional groups. Additionally, the N-GQDs-300 can efficiently promote the synthesis of MIL-101 which has an increased BET specific surface area (3000 m2/g), with the help of large amounts of multiple ROS. These as-obtained N-GQDs with strong chemical activity and efficient generation of a large amount of ROS show potential applications in organic and inorganic synthesis, catalytic reaction, or removal of organic contaminants.

Shuhan Cai: Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jingzi Beiyuan: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Liyun Huang: Investigation. Xiaocong Cao: Resources, Investigation. Maojun Deng: Validation, Investigation. Daofei Lv: Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Xin Chen: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Defei Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Dongmei Luo: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Wenbing Yuan: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge GuangDong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021A151501133), Foshan Engineering and Technology Research Center for Novel Porous Materials and Characteristic Innovation Research Project of University Teachers (2020XCC08), Foshan Science and Technology Innovation Project (2017AG100141), Guangdong natural resources foundation ([2020]036) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2022.118072.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary video 2
